select_when: Many agents are adapting at once, so the environment behaves more like a living system than a fixed machine.
Feedback, delays, and incentives keep reshaping the situation, including the effects of the intervention itself.
Improving the whole depends on understanding where a small change can alter learning loops, coordination, or adaptation.
The team keeps describing parts correctly but still misses the behavior emerging from their interaction.
avoid_when: Complexity language is being used as an alibi for not naming concrete leverage points, experiments, or ownership.
The model is being used to admire the system's complexity instead of deciding what variable, boundary, or loop actually matters next.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model centered on interdependence, adaptation, and system-level intervention choice rather than on simulation tooling sprawl.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor topology or simulation overlays because the raw source is already strongest on feedback-dominated adaptive systems and intervention choice.
Did not add higher-order fields because this batch remains inside the frozen Wave 1 to Wave 3 contracts.
open_questions: The output type compresses a source that spans system diagnosis, organizational adaptation, and foresight about emergent behavior.

select_when.note: The raw source is explicit that CAS is strongest when many agents adapt at once and feedback reshapes the environment over time.

avoid_when.note: The complexity-as-alibi warning is explicit; the second anti-activation line is conservative reviewed normalization of the same boundary.

input_type.note: The raw source activates on adaptive, multi-agent, feedback-heavy environments; the compact input label is reviewed normalization.

output_type.note: The source yields system maps and adaptive intervention frames; the output label compresses that into a typed artifact.

reasoning_types.note: Complex adaptive systems clearly require causal and systems reasoning, but the taxonomy labels remain reviewed normalization.
